what_you_will_learn: Distributed Tracing — the concept of tracing requests as they flow through a distributed system
Jaeger Architecture — understanding the components that make up Jaeger, including the Collector, Agent, and Query Service
Jaeger UI — using the web interface to visualize and explore traces, including filtering, grouping, and searching
Sampling Strategies — configuring sampling rates for different services and scenarios to balance data quality and volume
Jaeger API — using the API to query and manipulate traces programmatically, including creating custom dashboards and visualizations
Service Discovery — integrating Jaeger with service discovery tools like Kubernetes or Consul to automatically instrument services
Error Tracking — using Jaeger to identify and diagnose errors in distributed systems, including error propagation and correlation

knowledge.what_it_is: New Relic is an all-in-one observability platform that provides real-time insights into application performance, infrastructure, and user experience. It helps DevOps teams identify issues, optimize resources, and improve overall system reliability.
what_you_will_learn: Installing the New Relic agent for Java — configuring the Java agent to collect metrics and logs from your Java applications
Setting up APM (Application Performance Monitoring) dashboards — creating custom dashboards to monitor application performance, including response time, error rates, and throughput
Configuring alerting and notification policies — setting up alerts for critical issues and notifying teams via email or Slack
Using the New Relic UI to investigate issues — navigating the UI to identify root causes of performance problems and optimizing system resources
Integrating with CI/CD pipelines using NRQL (New Relic Query Language) — automating data collection and analysis in your CI/CD workflows
Monitoring database performance with New Relic One — tracking database metrics, including query latency, throughput, and resource utilization
Using the New Relic Synthetic monitoring feature to monitor user experience — simulating user interactions to identify issues with application availability and responsiveness

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenTelemetry is an open-source, vendor-agnostic observability framework that provides a unified API and SDKs for collecting, processing, and exporting telemetry data (metrics, logs, traces) from cloud-native applications. It's designed to work with popular monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger.
what_you_will_learn: Instrumenting applications using OpenTelemetry SDKs for Java, Python, Node.js, Go, and .NET
Configuring and using OpenTelemetry APIs for metrics (e.g., `otlp.metrics.v1.MetricsService`), logs, and traces
Understanding the concept of spans and how to use them to track application performance
Using OpenTelemetry's context propagation mechanism to share metadata between services
Configuring and using OpenTelemetry exporters for Prometheus, Jaeger, and Zipkin
Monitoring and troubleshooting applications with OpenTelemetry's built-in dashboards and visualizations
Integrating OpenTelemetry with popular DevOps tools like Kubernetes, Docker, and Ansible

knowledge.what_it_is: CloudSmith is an open-source, multi-cloud DevOps platform that enables teams to automate and manage infrastructure, applications, and services across multiple cloud providers. It provides a unified interface for provisioning, configuration management, and monitoring.
what_you_will_learn: Multi-cloud strategy — designing and implementing a hybrid cloud architecture using CloudSmith
Cloud provider integrations — configuring and managing AWS, Azure, GCP, and other major cloud providers within CloudSmith
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — writing Terraform configurations to provision infrastructure resources in CloudSmith
Configuration Management — using Ansible or Puppet to manage configuration drift and ensure consistency across environments
Monitoring and logging — integrating with tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack for real-time monitoring and log analysis
Service Orchestration — defining and managing service templates, workflows, and pipelines in CloudSmith
Security and Compliance — implementing security best practices, access controls, and compliance frameworks within CloudSmith
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) — automating build, test, and deployment processes using CloudSmith's CI/CD pipeline
Cloud Cost Optimization — using CloudSmith to monitor and optimize cloud resource utilization, reducing costs and improving efficiency

knowledge.what_it_is: Artifactory is a universal repository manager that enables DevOps teams to manage, share, and collaborate on software artifacts across the development lifecycle.
what_you_will_learn: Repository management — creating and managing repositories for storing and retrieving artifacts
Maven and Gradle integration — configuring Artifactory as a central Maven or Gradle repository
Package management — installing, updating, and removing packages using Artifactory's package manager
Dependency management — resolving and managing dependencies between applications and libraries
Artifacts promotion and deployment — automating the promotion and deployment of artifacts to different environments
Repository security and access control — configuring user roles, permissions, and authentication for secure artifact access
Audit logging and reporting — tracking and analyzing artifact usage, downloads, and uploads

knowledge.what_it_is: Nexus is a universal artifact repository manager developed by Sonatype, used to store and manage software components, libraries, and dependencies across multiple projects. It's designed to simplify dependency management, reduce version conflicts, and improve collaboration among teams.
what_you_will_learn: Repository creation — setting up Nexus repositories for storing and managing artifacts
Maven and Gradle integration — configuring Nexus as a central repository for Maven and Gradle builds
Proxying and caching — using Nexus to proxy and cache dependencies, reducing download times and improving build performance
Security and authentication — configuring Nexus security settings, including user management, role-based access control, and SSL/TLS encryption
Artifact versioning and promotion — understanding how Nexus manages artifact versions, including snapshot and release versions
Repository grouping and hierarchy — organizing repositories in a hierarchical structure to improve navigation and search functionality
Nexus IQ integration — using Nexus IQ to analyze and manage software dependencies, identify vulnerabilities, and enforce security policies

knowledge.what_it_is: FluxCD is an open-source, cloud-native Continuous Delivery (CD) tool that automates software deployments to Kubernetes clusters, providing a GitOps approach to infrastructure as code. It was created by Weaveworks and is now maintained by the CNCF.
what_you_will_learn: GitOps — managing infrastructure as code through Git repositories, with FluxCD acting as the CD tool
Kubernetes manifests — writing YAML/JSON files for deploying applications to Kubernetes clusters using FluxCD
Source-to-Image (S2I) — building container images from source code using FluxCD's S2I functionality
Helm charts — packaging and deploying applications to Kubernetes using Helm, integrated with FluxCD
FluxCD components — understanding the different parts of the FluxCD system, including the Source Controller, Kustomize, and Helm
Kustomize — a tool for managing Kubernetes configurations and deployments, used by FluxCD
Git repository management — setting up and managing Git repositories for storing infrastructure as code with FluxCD

knowledge.what_it_is: ArgoCD is an open-source, continuous delivery (CD) tool that automates the deployment of applications to Kubernetes clusters. It provides a GitOps-based workflow, allowing developers to manage infrastructure as code and automate rollouts.
what_you_will_learn: GitOps — managing infrastructure as code using Git repositories
Application CRDs — defining custom resource definitions for applications
Workflows — automating deployment pipelines with YAML or JSON files
Releases — tracking changes to applications across multiple environments
Canaries — rolling out new versions of applications incrementally
Rollbacks — reverting to previous versions of applications in case of issues
Source-to-Image (S2I) — building images from source code and deploying them to clusters
Application Sets — grouping related applications for easier management

knowledge.what_it_is: Istio is an open-source service mesh platform that provides a layer of abstraction and automation for microservices communication, security, and traffic management. It was originally developed by Google and is now maintained by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). Istio enables DevOps teams to manage complex service interactions, monitor performance, and enforce policies across multiple environments.
what_you_will_learn: Service mesh architecture — designing a scalable and secure communication layer for microservices
Istio components — understanding the role of Pilot, Citadel, Galley, and Mixer in the Istio architecture
Virtual services — defining service endpoints, protocols, and ports using Istio's configuration model
Destination rules — controlling traffic routing, load balancing, and circuit breaking for microservices
Service entries — registering external services with Istio for secure communication
Istio policies — enforcing security, rate limiting, and quota management across services
Traffic management — using Istio's built-in features for canary releases, A/B testing, and traffic shifting
Monitoring and logging — integrating Istio with Prometheus, Grafana, and other monitoring tools

knowledge.what_it_is: Consul is a popular service mesh and configuration management tool developed by HashiCorp, used to manage and discover services, connect microservices, and provide service discovery, health checking, and load balancing capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Service registration — registering services with Consul for automatic discovery and load balancing
Health checking — configuring Consul to monitor service health and trigger restarts or failovers
Service discovery — using Consul's DNS interface to resolve service names to IP addresses and ports
Load balancing — using Consul's built-in load balancing features to distribute traffic across multiple instances
Config management — storing and retrieving configuration data for services using Consul's key-value store
Secrets management — securely storing and retrieving sensitive data, such as API keys or credentials, with Consul's secrets engine
Multi-datacenter support — configuring Consul to manage services across multiple datacenters and regions
Service mesh integration — integrating Consul with service mesh tools like Istio or Linkerd for advanced traffic management

knowledge.what_it_is: Linkerd is a modern service mesh that provides traffic management, security, and observability for microservices-based applications. It was created by Buoyant and is now part of the CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation). Linkerd aims to simplify service communication and provide real-time insights into application performance.
what_you_will_learn: Service meshes — a layer of abstraction between services, enabling traffic management, security, and observability
Linkerd architecture — understanding the control plane, data plane, and proxy components
MTC (Microservices Traffic Control) — Linkerd's built-in traffic management system for routing and load balancing
Consul integration — using Consul as a service registry and configuration store with Linkerd
envoy proxies — configuring and managing Envoy proxies within the Linkerd data plane
Traffic shifting — gradual rollout of new versions or services using Linkerd's traffic management features
Fault injection — simulating failures to test application resilience with Linkerd's fault injection capabilities

knowledge.what_it_is: Envoy is an open-source service mesh built by Lyft, designed to manage and observe microservices communication in modern cloud-native applications. It provides features like load balancing, circuit breaking, and traffic management.
what_you_will_learn: Service Mesh Architecture — designing Envoy as a sidecar container to intercept and control service-to-service communication
Proxy Protocol — understanding how Envoy uses the proxy protocol to pass metadata between services
Virtual Service Routing — configuring Envoy to route traffic based on virtual services, headers, and other attributes
Rate Limiting and Circuit Breaking — implementing rate limiting and circuit breaking policies using Envoy's built-in features
Service Discovery — integrating Envoy with service discovery tools like Consul or etcd for automatic registration and deregistration of services
Traffic Management — using Envoy to manage traffic between services, including canary releases and blue-green deployments
Metrics and Observability — collecting metrics and logs from Envoy using tools like Prometheus and Grafana

knowledge.what_it_is: Availability refers to the measure of system uptime, ensuring that applications and services are accessible to users at all times. In DevOps, availability is critical for delivering high-quality software and meeting business objectives.
what_you_will_learn: High Availability Clustering — designing and implementing clusters for load balancing, failover, and scalability using tools like HAProxy, Keepalived, and Pacemaker
Autoscaling — dynamically adjusting resource allocation based on demand using AWS Auto Scaling, Google Cloud Autoscaling, or Azure autoscale
Load Balancing — distributing incoming traffic across multiple servers to improve responsiveness and availability with NGINX, Apache HTTP Server, or HAProxy
Failover and Failback — implementing automatic failover and manual failback procedures for disaster recovery using tools like MySQL Replication, PostgreSQL Streaming Replication, or Oracle Data Guard
Service Discovery — enabling services to discover and communicate with each other using DNS-based service discovery (e.g., etcd), Consul, or ZooKeeper
Circuit Breaker Pattern — implementing fault-tolerant designs that prevent cascading failures in microservices architectures using Hystrix, Resilience4J, or Netflix's Ribbon
Distributed Tracing — analyzing and visualizing request flows across multiple services to identify performance bottlenecks with tools like OpenTracing, Jaeger, or Zipkin

knowledge.what_it_is: Data management refers to the practices and tools used by DevOps teams to collect, store, process, and analyze data from various sources, ensuring efficient decision-making and continuous improvement. This involves leveraging cloud-based services like AWS Lake Formation, Google Cloud Data Catalog, or Azure Data Factory.
what_you_will_learn: Data Ingestion — using tools like Apache NiFi, AWS Kinesis, or Google Cloud Pub/Sub to collect data from various sources
Data Warehousing — designing and implementing data warehouses using Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, or Snowflake for efficient querying and analysis
Data Lake Management — managing data lakes with tools like Apache Hadoop, AWS Lake Formation, or Google Cloud Data Catalog for scalable storage and processing
ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) Pipelines — designing and implementing ETL pipelines using tools like Talend, Informatica PowerCenter, or AWS Glue for data integration and transformation

Data Quality and Governance — implementing data quality checks and governance policies using tools like Apache Airflow, AWS Lake Formation, or Google Cloud Data Catalog for ensuring data accuracy and compliance
Real-time Data Processing — processing real-time data streams using tools like Apache Flink, Apache Storm, or AWS Kinesis for fast decision-making and reaction to events

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud Design Patterns refer to reusable solutions that address common problems when designing cloud-based systems, providing a blueprint for architects and engineers to build scalable, secure, and efficient applications on cloud platforms.
what_you_will_learn: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) — designing microservices as independent services with clear interfaces and APIs
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — using events to trigger actions across multiple services, enabling loose coupling and scalability
Serverless Design Patterns — leveraging serverless computing for event-driven processing, real-time analytics, and batch processing
Containerization and Orchestration — using Docker, Kubernetes, and other tools to manage containerized applications and services
Service Mesh Architecture — implementing a service mesh to provide service discovery, load balancing, and traffic management
API Gateway Design Patterns — designing API gateways for secure, scalable, and efficient API exposure
Security and Compliance Patterns — implementing security measures such as encryption, access control, and auditing to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements

knowledge.what_it_is: Management and Monitoring in DevOps refers to the practices and tools used to ensure the smooth operation of infrastructure, applications, and services. It involves monitoring performance, identifying issues, and implementing corrective actions to minimize downtime and optimize resource utilization.
what_you_will_learn: Prometheus — a popular open-source monitoring system for collecting metrics from various sources
Grafana — a visualization tool for creating dashboards to display Prometheus metrics
Alertmanager — a tool for managing alerts generated by Prometheus, allowing for customization of notification channels and escalation policies
Service Discovery with Consul or etcd — understanding how service discovery works in containerized environments
Logging with Fluentd or Logstash — learning about log aggregation and processing pipelines
Monitoring Container Resource Utilization with cAdvisor or Node Exporter — understanding how to monitor CPU, memory, and disk usage of containers
Implementing SLAs (Service Level Agreements) and SLOs (Service Level Objectives) — defining and tracking performance targets for services
